      Musk Stuff
   =20
 =20
Last month, when Elon Musk first proposed to buy Twitter Inc. for $54.20 pe=
r share, Saudi Prince Alwaleed bin Talal, who owns about 4.6% of Twitter,  =
said no. He tweeted=C2=A0that Musk=E2=80=99s offer did not =E2=80=9Ccome cl=
ose to the intrinsic value=E2=80=9D of Twitter. (Musk replied by asking Alw=
aleed about his views on free speech.)Eventually Twitter=E2=80=99s board ap=
proved a merger at $54.20 per share. But last=C2=A0week, Musk announced tha=
t Alwaleed will not be taking the $54.20. His Kingdom Holding Co.=C2=A0has =
agreed to keep=C2=A0its shares and roll them over into=C2=A0the new, Musk-o=
wned Twitter. Incredibly, Elon Musk is finally going to take a public compa=
ny private with Saudi funding.The advantage of this for Alwaleed is that=C2=
=A0he thinks Twitter is worth more than $54.20 per share and gets to keep o=
wning Twitter. Sure he thought that a public, non-Musk-controlled Twitter w=
as worth more than $54.20, and now he is stuck owning a private Musk Twitte=
r, but if you believe Musk=E2=80=99s projections then Musk Twitter is worth=
 way=C2=A0more=C2=A0than regular Twitter.The advantage for Musk is mostly=
=C2=A0that it saves him some cash:=C2=A0Paying $54.20 per share to Alwaleed=
 for his 34.9 million shares would cost almost $1.9 billion in cash. Rollin=
g him over costs nothing. Well, no, actually it costs Musk something like 6=
% of whatever profits he ends up making on Twitter.[1] But it doesn=E2=80=
=99t cost him any=C2=A0cash, now, which probably matters.There is another a=
dvantage for Musk, which is that Alwaleed (1) owns 4.6% of Twitter=E2=80=99=
s stock and (2) thinks the deal price is too low. If he were=C2=A0not=C2=A0=
rolling over his stake into Musk Twitter, you might expect him to vote=C2=
=A0against=C2=A0the merger. But since he is now on Musk=E2=80=99s team, you=
=E2=80=99d expect him to vote=C2=A0for=C2=A0the merger.[2] I don't have muc=
h reason to think that many Twitter shareholders will vote against the deal=
 =E2=80=94=C2=A0the stock closed at $49.80 on Friday, suggesting that most =
shareholders disagree with Alwaleed that $54.20 is too low =E2=80=94=C2=A0b=
ut perhaps this does help lock in votes.Alwaleed=E2=80=99s plan to roll his=
 shares into Musk Twitter was disclosed in a filing last week describing eq=
uity commitments that Musk has gotten from investors who plan to join him i=
n owning the new, private Twitter. They total about $7.1 billion so far. Mu=
sk had  initially committed to put up, in essence, $33.5 billion of his own=
 money: $12.5 billion borrowed from banks against his Tesla Inc. stock, and=
 $21 billion of cash from other sources (presumably selling Tesla stock, et=
c.). The $7.1 billion from co-investors will be used to cut the margin loan=
 in half (to $6.25 billion) and, presumably, to slightly reduce the other $=
21 billion he has to come up with. The co-investors are sort of a random gr=
oup; there=E2=80=99s fellow billionaire Larry Ellison, venture firms like S=
equoia Capital and  Andreessen Horowitz, crypto firm  Binance, Qatar Holdin=
gs, and some asset managers who seem to be Elon Musk fans. There is also Fi=
delity Management & Research Co., which is an existing Twitter shareholder =
and seems to be effectively rolling some (though not all) of its public Twi=
tter shares into Musk Twitter.[3]The reporting on this, and Musk=E2=80=99s =
own filing, suggests that this is just the start, and that Musk =E2=80=9Cis=
 seeking and ... may receive additional financing commitments to fund addit=
ional portions of the total Merger Consideration,=E2=80=9D reducing both Mu=
sk=E2=80=99s ownership of Musk Twitter and the amount of cash he will need.=
 I don=E2=80=99t know how far this will go. Presumably Musk, the richest pe=
rson in the world, will put up many billions of dollars of his own money an=
d=C2=A0will own a majority of Musk Twitter.=C2=A0Still I can kind of imagin=
e him selling down most of his stake? I feel like there is an idealized mod=
el of an Elon Musk merger that would go like this:=09He shows up at some pu=
blic company.=09He suggests a price for that company. The price is somewhat=
 arbitrary, but it should (1) represent=C2=A0a premium to the current tradi=
ng price and (2) involve some funny number, ideally 420, which is a weed jo=
ke.=09People who already own shares of the company, and who=C2=A0like Elon =
Musk and want him to run the company, just keep their stock.=09People who=
=C2=A0don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0own shares of the company, but who=C2=A0like Elon =
Musk and want him to run the company and want to be a part of that, buy sto=
ck (at Musk=E2=80=99s somewhat arbitrary joke price).=09People who=C2=A0do=
=C2=A0own shares of the company, but who don=E2=80=99t particularly want to=
 be involved in the Elon Musk situation, sell stock (at Musk=E2=80=99s pric=
e).=09Ideally the orders in No. 4 and No. 5 would exactly balance, so that =
all the Musk fans can get in and all the Musk haters can get out. (I don=E2=
=80=99t know why this would work?)=09Then the company is 100% owned by fans=
 of Musk and they elect him chief executive officer, Technoking, Memelord, =
whatever.=09The stock continues to trade? I don=E2=80=99t know.This is an e=
xtremely vague sketch, and obviously most of the steps do not make any=C2=
=A0sense, but I nonetheless think it captures something essential about not=
 only Musk=E2=80=99s planned acquisition of Twitter but also about his 2018=
  pretend=C2=A0going-private transaction=C2=A0for Tesla Inc.=C2=A0Elon Musk=
 is not, I think, especially interested in=C2=A0owning=C2=A0Twitter. He is =
interested in=C2=A0controlling Twitter. The goal is not to make a financial=
 model that generates a 30% internal rate of return on Musk=E2=80=99s conce=
ntrated levered equity stake in Twitter. The goal is for Twitter to be=C2=
=A0(1) run by him (as CEO, initially, and I suppose eventually by some othe=
r CEO chosen by him) and (2) owned partly by him but mostly by a bunch of l=
oyal shareholders who will let him do what he wants.=C2=A0You can=E2=80=99t=
 really=C2=A0do=C2=A0the thing that I sketched out above, but you can do=C2=
=A0some=C2=A0of it. Musk is clearly=C2=A0open to current Twitter shareholde=
rs keeping their shares. He  said publicly that his goal =E2=80=9Cis to ret=
ain as many shareholders as is allowed by the law in a private company.=E2=
=80=9D=C2=A0So far only Alwaleed and Fidelity have=C2=A0signed on, but in t=
he same filing Musk said that he =E2=80=9Cis having, and will continue to h=
ave, discussions with certain existing holders of Common Stock (including J=
ack Dorsey) regarding the possibility of contributing such shares of Common=
 Stock to Parent, at or immediately prior to the closing of the Merger, in =
order to retain an equity investment in Twitter following completion of the=
 Merger in lieu of receiving Merger Consideration in the Merger.=E2=80=9D I=
f Dorsey =E2=80=94=C2=A0a Twitter founder, former CEO, current board member=
 =E2=80=94=C2=A0or anyone else wants to sign up for Musk Twitter, then they=
=E2=80=99re welcome, to the extent allowed by law. In rough terms, what tha=
t means is that any billionaire or institutional investor who wants to sign=
 up with Musk can do so, but Musk=E2=80=99s retail-investor fans mostly can=
=E2=80=99t. Musk Twitter will be a private company, meaning that it can=E2=
=80=99t have a lot of retail shareholders.Also, in my sketch above, I assum=
e that Musk sets some joke price =E2=80=94=C2=A0here, $54.20 =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0and that price magically balances supply and demand. It would be weird i=
f that were true, and so far, for Twitter, it isn=E2=80=99t. As of right no=
w, Musk has about $7.1 billion of equity commitments to buy Twitter at $54.=
20, much less than the $40+ billion that Twitter=E2=80=99s board has commit=
ted to sell him at that price.[4] The rest of the money will have to come f=
rom some combination of=C2=A0(1) him hustling up some more investors (still=
 a live effort), (2) somewhat unhealthy amounts of debt financing raised ag=
ainst Twitter and (3) his own pocket. Which is fine, really; he=E2=80=99s v=
ery rich. But clearly he=E2=80=99d rather buy Twitter without paying for it=
.I used to make fun of Musk for this model, this idea that he can take a co=
mpany private while keeping any shareholder who wants to stick around. And =
I stand by that, in the sense that it does not really work as a matter of m=
ergers-and-acquisitions law or securities law or even, like, the meaning of=
 the word =E2=80=9Cprivate.=E2=80=9D But now I kind of see his point. The p=
oint is that if you take a public company that is mostly owned by dispersed=
 diversified shareholders who are not directly involved in the management o=
f the company, and you transform the shareholder list so that it is now own=
ed=C2=A0by dispersed diversified shareholders who are not directly involved=
 in the management of the company and=C2=A0who all love Elon Musk, then you=
 can arguably create value. You create the value by putting Elon Musk in ch=
arge, letting him do whatever he wants, and hoping that it will work. So fa=
r it often has! You transform a regular public company into an Elon Musk pa=
ssion project, and maybe everyone gets rich.This never made any sense as a =
reason to take Tesla private, because Tesla was=C2=A0already=C2=A0owned by =
shareholders who love Elon Musk, so that never went anywhere. But as a stra=
tegy for Twitter, sure, why not.An alternative approach here would be=C2=A0=
activism:=C2=A0Musk could buy 9% of Twitter, say =E2=80=9CI want to meddle =
with Twitter now,=E2=80=9D run a proxy fight to replace the board with his =
handpicked nominees, try to get support from other shareholders, and ultima=
tely in effect take control of the company without buying all of it for cas=
h. Lots of activist investors do things like this, and when Musk first boug=
ht his stake in Twitter a month ago, that=E2=80=99s kind of  what people ex=
pected. He was  given a board seat almost immediately. But he seems to have=
 decided, I think reasonably, that this sort of activism would be slower an=
d more restrictive and give him less control than just buying the company a=
nd redistributing it to more loyal shareholders.This also helps explain Mus=
k=E2=80=99s apparent plan=C2=A0to take Twitter public again in three years.=
 I  wrote last week:If, as Musk sometimes suggests, it is bad for =E2=80=9C=
free speech=E2=80=9D or whatever for Twitter to be a public company beholde=
n to shareholders, what changes in three years? Is it that when he takes Tw=
itter public again, Musk will remain the majority shareholder? Will it have=
 dual-class stock? Will a re-IPO=E2=80=99ed Musk Twitter just be sort of Mu=
sky enough, with Musk-fan retail shareholders and a Musk-aligned board of d=
irectors, that it can withstand any pressures on free speech?=C2=A0=E2=80=
=9CThis is the town square, an important venue for public communication, so=
 the richest man in the world must take it private=E2=80=9D always struck m=
e as a very weird argument, but =E2=80=9C=E2=80=A6 and then take it public =
again almost immediately=E2=80=9D adds some new complications.But I guess t=
he point here is that he's not=C2=A0really=C2=A0taking it private. Or he is=
, but not in the sense that he will own it privately; it will be owned by a=
 bunch of big institutional investors, but he=E2=80=99ll be in control. He=
=E2=80=99s adjusting the shareholder register to make it Muskier. After tha=
t, sure, he can take it public again and retain control; it has worked for =
Tesla.I=C2=A0want to make one other point about this approach, which is tha=
t it is awkward for Twitter=E2=80=99s board of directors, who approved this=
 merger essentially because they concluded that $54.20 per share is vastly =
more than Twitter is worth. Imagine a limit case where Musk convinces holde=
rs of 51% of the stock to roll over into Musk Twitter.[5] The merger still =
has to be approved by a vote of Twitter shareholders. In that limit case:=
=09A majority of Twitter shareholders would think that Twitter is worth mor=
e than $54.20 per share (which is why they are keeping their stock), but=09=
They=E2=80=99d all vote to approve the merger and cash out the=C2=A0other=
=C2=A0shareholders at $54.20.The continuing shareholders would force out th=
e minority at a price that they themselves think is too low. Seems bad! Obv=
iously that is not where we are =E2=80=94=C2=A0so far only about two and a =
half shareholders (Musk, Alwaleed, some of Fidelity) plan to roll into Musk=
 Twitter, and most Twitter shareholders seem to think that $54.20 is quite=
=C2=A0generous =E2=80=94=C2=A0but there is already something awkward about =
Twitter=E2=80=99s biggest shareholders (and a board member!) being pitched =
on keeping their stock and voting to cash out the regular shareholders.One =
way to address this  would have been for Twitter=E2=80=99s board to insist =
on some sort of majority-of-the-minority vote, where the merger could only =
happen if a majority of the shareholders who are=C2=A0not=C2=A0part of Musk=
=E2=80=99s buying group voted to approve it. The board  did not demand that=
, but it  did not demand a lot of things. Intriguingly,=C2=A0one=C2=A0share=
holder=C2=A0is demanding it:Elon Musk=E2=80=99s $44 billion buyout of Twitt=
er Inc. was challenged in a lawsuit by a Florida pension fund that argues t=
he deal can=E2=80=99t close before 2025 because Musk was an =E2=80=9Cintere=
sted shareholder=E2=80=9D in the social-networking platform.The Orlando Pol=
ice Pension Fund filed suit in Delaware Chancery Court on Thursday. Accordi=
ng to the complaint, Musk had agreements with other major Twitter sharehold=
ers -- including founder Jack Dorsey -- to rely on their holdings when offe=
ring to take the company private last month. Those arrangements triggered a=
 Delaware law that calls for a three-year delay in closing such deals, the =
fund claims. ...Under Delaware corporate law, those agreements make Musk an=
 =E2=80=9Cinterested shareholder=E2=80=9D who has to wait three years to cl=
ose the deal or win the support of investors who control =E2=80=9Cat least =
66 2/3% of Twitter=E2=80=99s outstanding voting stock=E2=80=9D and were ind=
ependent from the billionaire, the suit said.This, I think, is probably not=
 right. (The relevant law is Delaware General Corporation Law section 203, =
and particularly the definition of =E2=80=9Cownership=E2=80=9D in section 2=
03(c)(9)(iii).)=C2=A0There is no indication that Musk has any sort of votin=
g agreement with Dorsey (though Dorsey seems enthusiastic=C2=A0about the de=
al), and it misunderstands the role of Morgan Stanley as Musk=E2=80=99s inv=
estment banker and also as an investment adviser to funds that own Twitter.=
 On the other hand, does Musk now have an arrangement with=C2=A0Alwaleed? I=
f he signs up Dorsey to roll over his shares, are they part of a group for =
these purposes?Anyway, also last week, the New York Times reported on Musk=
=E2=80=99s plans for Twitter, which include growing revenue to $26.4 billio=
n and users to 931 million by 2028, while also charging for subscriptions a=
nd reducing reliance on advertising. It all seems a little optimistic but t=
he man runs a space rocket company, what do I know. Meanwhile: =E2=80=9CAt =
a Twitter Inc. staff meeting Wednesday morning, the first slide of a presen=
tation asked a question on the minds of many employees: =E2=80=98Why Bother=
?=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D As a corporate finance matter, Musk is reshuffling Twit=
ter=E2=80=99s shareholder base to make it Muskier, but as a management matt=
er=C2=A0he might have to do the same with the employees.

   =20
 =20
I dunno. Yesterday the chief executive officer of Uber Technologies Inc.,=
=C2=A0Dara Khosrowshahi, sent out an email to employees telling them that U=
ber is going to try to make money from doing its businesses:=E2=80=9CWe hav=
e to make sure our unit economics work before we go big,=E2=80=9D the Uber =
boss wrote. =E2=80=9CThe least efficient marketing and incentive spend will=
 be pulled back.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6Uber will now focus on achieving profita=
bility on a free cash flow basis rather than adjusted earnings before inter=
est, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, Khosrowshahi said.There are arg=
uably two basic models of how to build a big business:=09Try to do somethin=
g that makes money. Then try to do more of it.=09Try to do as much as possi=
ble of something that=C2=A0loses=C2=A0money. Then try to make it make money=
.The second model was arguably  the major business paradigm of the last dec=
ade; we have talked about it under names like =E2=80=9Cthe MoviePass econom=
y=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cblitzscaling.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0Perhaps no company was =
more associated with it than Uber. And now Uber is changing to the first mo=
del, =E2=80=9Cmake sure our unit economics work before we go big.=E2=80=9DI=
n one sense, there is nothing surprising about a public-company CEO telling=
 employees that (1) they should try to make money and (2) if they are doing=
 things that lose money, they should do=C2=A0less=C2=A0of them rather than=
=C2=A0more. In another sense, this is the CEO of=C2=A0Uber.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CI=
t=E2=80=99s clear that the market is experiencing a seismic shift,=E2=80=9D=
 says Khosrowshahi,=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cand we need to react accordingly.=E2=80=
=9DAnyway stocks were down last week; the S&P 500 index has gone down each =
of the last five weeks, and things seem especially rough in the tech indust=
ry. In a world where money is cheap and plentiful, losing a lot of it for t=
he foreseeable future in order to grab a share of huge faraway profits can =
be a reasonable strategy, or at least one that can attract investors. In a =
world of higher discount rates, making money now is distinctly preferable t=
o losing it. A seismic shift.Elsewhere,=C2=A0the meme stocks are over!Nursi=
ng losses in 2022 that are worse than the rest of the market=E2=80=99s, ama=
teur investors who jumped in when the lockdown began have now given back al=
l of their once-prodigious gains, according to an estimate by Morgan Stanle=
y. The calculation is based on trades placed by new entrants since the star=
t of 2020 and uses exchange and public price-feed data to tally overall pro=
fits and losses. =E2=80=A6=E2=80=9CA lot of these guys started trading righ=
t around Covid so their only investing experience was the wacked-out, Fed-f=
ueled market,=E2=80=9D said Matthew Tuttle, chief executive officer at Tutt=
le Capital Management LLC. =E2=80=9CThat all changed with the Fed pivot in =
November, but they didn=E2=80=99t realize that because they have never seen=
 a market that wasn=E2=80=99t supported by the Fed,=E2=80=9D he said. =E2=
=80=9CThe results have been horrific.=E2=80=9D ...Famous names from the hei=
ght of the frenzy are nursing serious losses. AMC Entertainment Inc. is dow=
n 78% since June 2021. It=E2=80=99s lost 49% this year. Peloton Interactive=
 Inc. is off 90% from its record. From the start of 2020 to last November, =
a basket of retail =C2=A0stock favored by retail trades that Goldman Sachs =
Group Inc. tracks more than doubled. This year, that basket has plunged 32%=
, more than twice the S&P 500=E2=80=99s decline.=C2=A0I don=E2=80=99t know,=
 man, GameStop Corp. closed at $114.70 on Friday. It was down another 10% (=
!) as of noon today, but still. It started 2021 at $18.84, and we all  lost=
 our minds when it hit $76.79. If your only meme-stock trade was being earl=
y to the main meme-stock trade, you=E2=80=99re still doing okay.  Meanwhile=
:The surge in individual stock trading by Americans last year contributed t=
o a record tax haul for the federal government this spring. =E2=80=A6Indivi=
dual taxes from small business proceeds or the sale of stock and other asse=
ts are nearly triple the 2019 levels.Tax preparers, in the annual season th=
at closed last month, had reported a significant increase among their clien=
ts of people using services like Robinhood. Close watchers of Treasury data=
 picked up on similar trends.=E2=80=9CA big chunk of it has come from short=
-term capital gains,=E2=80=9D said Lou Crandall, chief economist at Wrights=
on ICAP LLC. =E2=80=9CMeme stocks were very, very good to the IRS.=E2=80=9D=
The short-term capital=C2=A0loss rules=C2=A0are less generous, so if you ma=
de a million dollars trading last year and lost it all this year your tax s=
ituation will feel unfair.

   =20
 =20
If you are good at investing, how hard can X be, for any X? There is a stan=
dard story of hedge-fund-manager hubris, in which:=09You start a small hedg=
e fund focused on some narrow niche.=09You do well,=C2=A0racking up consist=
ent high returns for a few years.=09You decide you are an all-around invest=
ing genius; you raise more money and expand your fund into other areas.=09A=
lso you decide you are an all-around=C2=A0everything=C2=A0genius and start =
telling corporate managers, politicians, teachers, scientists, etc., how to=
 do their jobs.=09Possibly you are wrong about one or both of these things,=
 but if No. 3 works out well enough no one ever tells you you=E2=80=99re wr=
ong about No. 4.On the other hand here is a story about how Steve Cohen, wh=
o is very good at managing a (large and multi-strategy) hedge fund, looked =
at Major League Baseball, thought =E2=80=9Chow hard can this possibly be,=
=E2=80=9D bought a team, put his hedge fund in charge of it, and =E2=80=A6=
=C2=A0it =E2=80=A6 worked?Some senior employees from Cohen=E2=80=99s firm, =
Point72 Asset Management, have been moonlighting as Mets employees in cruci=
al roles, according to staffers=E2=80=99 LinkedIn pages, the Mets=E2=80=99 =
website and people familiar with the matter. Many are tasked with improving=
 the team=E2=80=99s once-outdated infrastructure, data-analytics capabiliti=
es and technological prowess. =E2=80=A6Since Cohen took over, the number of=
 people working in data and analytics has grown from eight full-time employ=
ees to 35, some of them from Point72, a person familiar with the matter sai=
d. Cohen has embedded data scientists in the Mets=E2=80=99 scouting departm=
ents. A new data-engineering group is also stocked with people from Point72=
.These investments are helping to make the Mets one of the best teams in ba=
seball, where they are seen as significant World Series contenders this sea=
son. Point72 is a key reason why.There are limits: =E2=80=9CThe Point72 emp=
loyees aren=E2=80=99t the ones deciding which players the Mets should acqui=
re or how they should deploy their bullpen,=E2=80=9D and they are not, you =
know, actually playing baseball themselves. (True hedge-fund-manager hubris=
 would be saying =E2=80=9Chey that man is just throwing a ball, how hard ca=
n that be=E2=80=9D and putting yourself on the roster.) Still this is an ex=
ample of a hedge fund manager looking at a high-profile, mature and competi=
tive field, saying =E2=80=9Cah sure I could do that,=E2=80=9D parachuting i=
n and in fact being successful. I guess he should run for governor next.On =
the other hand:Several Point72 investors contacted by The Wall Street Journ=
al in recent weeks said they were unhappy about the moonlighting or unaware=
 of it altogether. Some said they withdrew their money from Point72 after C=
ohen bought the team partly because they viewed him as distracted by the Me=
ts.This seems misguided to me, but I write about Elon Musk all the time now=
 so I suppose my standards are all messed up. =E2=80=9CWhat, he runs=C2=A0t=
wo=C2=A0complicated organizations, that=E2=80=99s nothing,=E2=80=9D is my i=
nstinctive thought. Also the goal of modern management of high-intensity or=
ganizations often seems to be to merge work with every aspect of the employ=
ees=E2=80=99 lives; if Cohen=E2=80=99s employees are doing data science at =
their job (as hedge-fund data scientists) and then also at their hobby (as =
baseball data scientists) then really that means they have fewer=C2=A0distr=
actions.=C2=A0Also:Cohen=E2=80=99s chief-of-staff, Michael Sullivan, said C=
ohen hasn=E2=80=99t missed a day of trading since he bought the Mets and co=
ntinues to work seven days a week.=E2=80=9CSome members of his senior leade=
rship team that were hoping he=E2=80=99d be distracted by the Mets on weeke=
nds have been horribly disappointed,=E2=80=9D Sullivan said.Oh man do I kno=
w that feeling. You have an always-on boss, something nice happens in his p=
ersonal life, you think =E2=80=9Cah now he will spend more time with his [f=
amily, sports team, etc.]=E2=80=9D and, nope, somehow he has even more time=
 to create work for you!Anyway I assume that if the Mets lose five games in=
 a row their payroll will be slashed. They=E2=80=99re a pod in a multistrat=
egy hedge fund now; that is just how it works.

greeing to roll into Musk Twitter if the merger happens anyway. You might e=
xpect Musk to demand some sort of voting agreement in which Alwaleed promis=

waleed=E2=80=99s filings), and I can imagine reasons why they would not wan=

chnically it is listed with the other co-investors who are contributing cas=

 commitment is a strange number =E2=80=94=C2=A0$316,139,386 =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
and all of Musk=E2=80=99s other cash commitments are reasonably round numbe=

g his shares, while Fidelity is getting cashed out for 100% of its shares a=

 entity than Kingdom Holdings and I assume it has to do with some sort of F=
idelity internal accounting concerns.=C2=A0[4] Don=E2=80=99t take that numb=

bout $41.4 billion at the merger price, though Musk already owns 73 million=

. (Arguably in a perfect Musk Merger he wouldn=E2=80=99t need to own any sh=

d warrants, etc., that increase the total consideration; his total financin=
g commitments are for $46.5 billion.[5] He=E2=80=99s currently at like =E2=
=80=A6=C2=A015%? He and Alwaleed combine for 14.1%, and Fidelity=E2=80=99s =
commitment amounts to about another 0.8%. Jack Dorsey, who seems to be a to=
p target,=C2=A0owns about 2.4%, and otherwise the shareholders seem pretty =
dispersed, so 51% seems very unlikely; this is a thought experiment, not a =

ottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Musk announced</a> that Alwale=

 16px 0;">The advantage of this for Alwaleed is that=C2=A0he thinks Twitter=
 is worth more than $54.20 per share and gets to keep owning Twitter. Sure =
he thought that a <em>public</em>, non-Musk-controlled Twitter was worth mo=
re than $54.20, and now he is stuck owning a <em>private</em> Musk Twitter,=
 but if you <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/276522=
54.263132/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnl0aW1lcy5jb20vMjAyMi8wNS8wNi90ZWNobm9sb2d5L2Vsb=

r: #ccc;">believe Musk=E2=80=99s projections</a> then Musk Twitter is worth=
 way=C2=A0<em>more=C2=A0</em>than regular Twitter.</p><p style=3D"margin: 1=
6px 0;">The advantage for Musk is mostly=C2=A0that it saves him some cash:=
=C2=A0Paying $54.20 per share to Alwaleed for his 34.9 million shares would=
 cost almost $1.9 billion in cash. Rolling him over costs nothing. Well, no=
, actually it costs Musk something like 6% of whatever profits he ends up m=
aking on Twitter.[1] But it doesn=E2=80=99t cost him any=C2=A0<em>cash</em>=
, now, which probably matters.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There is ano=
ther advantage for Musk, which is that Alwaleed (1) owns 4.6% of Twitter=E2=
=80=99s stock and (2) thinks the deal price is too low. If he were=C2=A0<em=
>not=C2=A0</em>rolling over his stake into Musk Twitter, you might expect h=
im to vote=C2=A0<em>against=C2=A0</em>the merger. But since he is now on Mu=
sk=E2=80=99s team, you=E2=80=99d expect him to vote=C2=A0<em>for=C2=A0</em>=
the merger.[2] I don't have much reason to think that many Twitter sharehol=
ders will vote against the deal =E2=80=94=C2=A0the stock closed at $49.80 o=
n Friday, suggesting that most shareholders disagree with Alwaleed that $54=
.20 is too low =E2=80=94=C2=A0but perhaps this does help lock in votes.</p>=

or: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> initiall=

5 billion borrowed from banks against his Tesla Inc. stock, and $21 billion=

. The $7.1 billion from co-investors will be used to cut the margin loan in=
 half (to $6.25 billion) and, presumably, to slightly reduce the other $21 =
billion he has to come up with. The co-investors are sort of a random group=

oia Capital and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27=

=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The reporting on this, and Musk=E2=80=99s own filing, =
suggests that this is just the start, and that Musk =E2=80=9Cis seeking and=

 ownership of Musk Twitter and the amount of cash he will need. I don=E2=80=
=99t know how far this will go. Presumably Musk, the richest person in the =
world, will put up many billions of dollars of his own money and=C2=A0will =
own a majority of Musk Twitter.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still=
 I can kind of imagine him selling down most of his stake? I feel like ther=
e is an idealized model of an Elon Musk merger that would go like this:</p>=
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">He shows up at some public company.</=
li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">He suggests a price for that compan=
y. The price is somewhat arbitrary, but it should (1) represent=C2=A0a prem=
ium to the current trading price and (2) involve some funny number, ideally=
 420, which is a weed joke.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">People=
 who already own shares of the company, and who=C2=A0like Elon Musk and wan=
t him to run the company, just keep their stock.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin=
-bottom: 5px;">People who=C2=A0<em>don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</em>own shares of th=
